title: Morning glory muffins
subtitle: American spiced muffins with carrot, apple, and pineapple
excerpt: Large, domed American muffins packed with carrot, apple, and desiccated coconut, spiced with cinnamon and ginger, enriched with oil for a moist crumb that stays fresh for days. A complete breakfast in a muffin, developed in a Nantucket café in the 1970s.
sourceNotes: Morning glory muffins were developed by Pam McKinstry at the Morning Glory Café on Nantucket, Massachusetts in the 1970s and published in Bon Appétit magazine in 1981, which brought them to a wide audience. The combination of carrot, apple, coconut, and raisins in an oil-enriched batter is the defining formula. The recipe here synthesises the public-domain tradition of oil-enriched spiced fruit muffins documented in American domestic baking literature from the mid-twentieth century, adapted to the combination of ingredients that defines the morning glory type.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze individually wrapped once cool. Thaw at room temperature for 2 hours, or microwave from frozen for 1 minute.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 24. Use two muffin tins.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stay moist for three days at room temperature, wrapped. Improve on the second day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. Start at a higher heat if you want a pronounced dome; reduce to 170°C after 5 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Mixing wet and dry ingredients separately and combining them with as few strokes as possible — typically 10 to 15 — until just combined. The batter will look lumpy; that is correct. Overmixing develops gluten and produces a tight, tunnelled crumb. The muffin method produces a tender, open crumb with a rounded top.

content.text: and an oil-enriched batter, both of which contribute to their characteristic moist crumb. The batter looks as though it has too much going on (carrot, apple, coconut, raisins), but the extra moisture from the vegetables and fruit is what keeps the muffins fresh for several days. Grate the carrot and apple on the coarse side of a box grater; squeeze the apple lightly in a cloth to remove excess juice.

attrs.prepNote: peeled and grated, excess juice squeezed out

content.text: Heat the oven to 190°C conventional. Line a 12-hole muffin tin with paper cases or grease each well. Whisk together the flour, sugar, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda, cinnamon, ginger, and salt in a large bowl. Add the grated carrot, apple, coconut, and raisins and stir to distribute through the flour. In a separate bowl, whisk together the eggs, oil, and vanilla. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and fold together using the muffin method — 12 to 15 firm strokes with a spatula until just combined. The batter will be thick and lumpy.

Divide between the cases, filling each one almost to the top. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the tops are deep gold, the centres are set when pressed, and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. They improve on standing and are at their best after a few hours.
